cards.question: What are the six links of the 2025 AHA universal Chain of Survival?
cards.answer: Activation of emergency response, high-quality CPR, defibrillation, advanced resuscitation interventions, post-cardiac arrest care, and recovery.

cards.question: Which link of the chain does the ACLS provider primarily own?
cards.answer: Link 4 — advanced resuscitation interventions (airway management, IV/IO access, rhythm-specific therapy, medications, treatment of reversible causes).

cards.question: What is the main systems-of-care emphasis for OHCA?
cards.answer: Bystander recognition and CPR, dispatcher-assisted instructions, public-access AED use, and rapid EMS response.

cards.question: What is the main systems-of-care emphasis for IHCA?
cards.answer: Surveillance and prevention — continuous monitoring, early-warning scores, and rapid-response/medical emergency teams that catch deterioration before full arrest.

cards.question: What capabilities define a regionalized cardiac arrest center?
cards.answer: 24/7 coronary angiography/PCI capability, targeted temperature management, multimodal neuroprognostication, and ECPR capability when indicated.

cards.question: Why should advanced interventions never come at the cost of compression fraction?
cards.answer: Because high-quality, minimally-interrupted CPR and early defibrillation remain the strongest predictors of survival; advanced care is additive, not a substitute.

cards.question: Name three metrics tracked in Utstein-style CQI reporting.
cards.answer: Any three of: chest compression fraction, time to first shock, time to first epinephrine dose, ROSC rate, survival to discharge, neurologic outcome.

cards.question: Why is post-cardiac arrest care described as a 'continuation' of resuscitation rather than a separate phase?
cards.answer: Because the same anticipatory, team-based mindset and treatment of reversible causes carries forward after ROSC into airway, hemodynamic, and temperature management — it is not the finish line.

cards.question: What system tool helps prevent in-hospital cardiac arrest before it happens?
cards.answer: Early-warning scores paired with rapid-response or medical emergency teams that respond to deteriorating patients.

cards.question: What is the purpose of debriefing after a resuscitation?
cards.answer: To identify system and team performance gaps using real data, feeding lessons back into training and protocols (part of continuous quality improvement).

cards.question: What replaced the previously separate adult/pediatric and IHCA/OHCA chains in 2025?
cards.answer: A single universal six-link Chain of Survival, with a separate Neonatal Chain of Care for newborns.

cards.question: What is the sixth link of the Chain of Survival and what does it include?
cards.answer: Recovery — rehabilitation, psychological support, treatment planning, and support for survivors and families.

cards.question: What analogous systems of care are cardiac arrest centers modeled after?
cards.answer: Trauma systems and stroke systems of care, which regionalize time-critical patients to centers with specialized capability.

cards.question: What should never be delayed by attempts at advanced airway or IV/IO access?
cards.answer: Chest compressions and defibrillation — advanced procedures must be fit around, not instead of, high-quality CPR.

cards.question: Give an example of a rhythm-appropriate ACLS therapy that begins at Link 4.
cards.answer: Examples include defibrillation/cardioversion timing refinement, epinephrine and antiarrhythmic administration, and advanced airway placement with capnography.

cards.question: Why is team-based, multidisciplinary care emphasized in ACLS?
cards.answer: Resuscitation requires simultaneous tasks (compressions, airway, drugs, documentation, family communication) that no single provider can perform alone, so defined roles and closed-loop communication are essential.
